speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. Dr. Perl, you were just explaining to me about dictating something that you had presented to Eichmann. And you wanted to share with me some of the translation which you have in front of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it is essential. Because unless the viewer of this video knows what is in this, the essence of [INAUDIBLE], they will not understand why the Germans cooperated with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, sir. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say that the situation is basically different now from the past emigrations which you have shown as it existed in Nazi-- in Germany controlled by the National Socialists. The overflow by Jewish emigration since 1933 and the likely imminent mass emigration from Spain are causing the countries of the world to tighten their borders, so that the Jews cannot emigrate. There are essential differences in the need to handle the Jewish emigration for Germany than there were before, the answer was Austria. The social stratification of Jews in Austria, in Deutsche Osterreich, in German Austria, is very different from the one in the old [NON-ENGLISH], Germany itself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Vienna there are almost 10% of the population Jewish, many of them in leading social positions. And in Germany, they are distributed all over the country. In Austria they are concentrated almost entirely in Vienna. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Due to the Anschluss, Germany has become a country of a substantial number of Jews and Jewish inhabitants. Therefore, the system needs some new organization, some new orientation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There is a utilization of related stratification to Palestine. To enter Palestine, you need today a certificate from the British that we are entitled to. The British hold this number of certificates to a minimum in order to continue to enjoy the favor of the Arabs. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But from each number of certificates issued-- and this is very important-- a number is retained by the British government for those who entered Palestine without official permission. I tried to make it clear that the illegal immigration is not really illegal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As the percentage of retained certificates is figured from the worldwide immigration number, the number of those delayed certificates is most of the time substantial. Jews of countries with a large Jewish population are making extensive use of this legal possibility to obtain one certificate. And that's legalizing their status after having first entered Palestine without official certification. Germany has so far made no use of this possibility, while other countries are making extensive use of it. This was not really true. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We possess-- we have organized such immigration, which we call visa-less or free immigration, for some time and have recently landed just two ships, with-- and I give the number of the ships. But this is nothing compared to what we can do. We can in a short time, bring at least 5,000 Jews from Vienna to Palestine without any problems for the Jews or for the German government. And all we need for this is that we are not-- do not find any impediments by the Germans through this. This and this in more detail is the content of the memorandum, where essentially I said what I said now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were dictating basically this memorandum.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Eichmann Memorandum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I dictated this memorandum, plus to achieve this, we need in one way the cooperation of the Reichsschatzministerium of the Treasury, of you. I said of you, the Reichsschatzministerium. We need the permission to transform some local currency into foreign currency. Because the Greeks, with whom we are dealing, won't-- do not accept German marks. They want pounds or dollars. And this is what I am here for. I told-- well--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said that you were there for the currency. But what you were there also for was to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only for the currency.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the important thing. That I told-- I first did not know where to go. I came to somebody. He just said no. It was an impressive title in [INAUDIBLE]. I told him, we are here from Vienna in order to make some details regarding mass emigration of Vienna from Jews to Palestine. Germany was full with happiness then that all the Jews, we'll be able to get rid of them now in Austria. It was a very big thing now in everybody's mind. So this man told us, I have nothing to do with it. You go to so and so. And we went to so and so, and talked to Mr. so and so, and so and so sends us to you. And we had already the point of reference. And we are sent from Vienna to work out some detail for the necessary functioning of this. They believed that we were sent by the Germans. And I didn't say that we were sent by the Germans. I just said, we are sent from Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here the second man was already closer to the issue. And he sent us to a third man, who was the man who was very sympathetic. He was-- saw himself as a big man who started the big emigration of Jews, and he permitted. He says, [NON-ENGLISH], which is always the German answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Put it down in writing. And I went across the street to the [NON-ENGLISH], and edit to the Eichmann memorandum that we need money, 12 pounds, which was $16. The entire time I spoke of pounds only. Because in the shipping, well you figure it in pounds, 12 pounds a person. And I came back to him on the day which he told me I should be back. I think we had come to him on a Tuesday. I was back on Thursday or Friday. And I got the permission for the first, I think, 5,000 people. Anyhow, we couldn't have been prouder that we got, I think, 2,000 pounds. 2,000 pounds to transfer. And he was careful. But he gave us the permission. And with this, we came home and started now to collect more passports, and to say we are about to leave. And Silberschein left with the 2,000 pounds for Athens to get a ship. Silberschein did not report soon. So we were panic-stricken. Maybe he stole the money. It was huge amount of money. But in the meanwhile, I was arrested. There was a day in Vienna, the first day of an action, police action, that they took Jews from the street. Jews whose names were outside like Dr. so and so, and [? General ?] so and so. And arrest somebody, lawyer so and so. And they got all these people, and other Jews until they had the quota, whatever the quota was, which was maybe 10,000 Jews. I don't know how many were arrested. But I was among the arrested, and Turi Deutsche was arrested. Turi Deutsche was arrested one day before me. After Turi Deutsche had been arrested, we had a meeting in the home of the current leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what should we do? Should we proceed with the action or not? And we don't know whether Turi was arrested because he's just a Jew, or because of the whole affair that they woke up to it that we have tricked the people in Berlin. And we decided to go on anyhow. So-- Tell me about your arrest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I then went to my office, and the girl told me there was somebody here from the police that you have to report to the police. And I went. And with the plan to tell all this, if possible. But I thought maybe, maybe it's just that I'm arrested like Turi was arrested, or like others were arrested. Just as a Jew. But I thought I would have to take a chance. I didn't have to go to the police. I could have been hiding at least for some time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to the police. And the police kept me there. I was formally introduced by a Austrian policeman who asked me if a few simple questions. Have you ever had an affair with a gentile girl? Have you ever sued a gentile for interest? Or have you ever taken interest for payment from [? German? ?] Some of the crimes the Jews are supposed to have committed. So I said, no, no, no, no. I sit there.

speaker: interviewee
text: With me, two days was the interview. It was not with me [INAUDIBLE], afterward, when I was still waiting outside for the formalities to be released. No. For [INAUDIBLE], it was not release. I was-- yes, yes I know now. I was sent down into the basement. Because it was a school. It was an emergency prison. A prison for [INAUDIBLE], for they used this school in the Karajan. K-A-R-A-J-A-N, Karajan, and it's strictly Jewish district. And there I was. And Turi I know had been arrested the day before. And the main thing for me now was to let the people know that I was not arrested because of the transport. They should go on with that. That rather I was arrested just as any other Jew. And there was all kinds of windows high up in the room, in the basement. It was a school basement. And we wrote notes, several of us, and I wrote two notes, one to my brother, and one to [? Eric ?] [? Wolfe, ?] in which I said, continue with the action. My arrest has nothing to do with the action.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you deliver this? You saw people walking by, and you saw a guard marching by slowly. So we figured how long it takes the guard to go to the corner and come back, for the time shortly after he marched in one direction was the time when we could send a paper out if somebody came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a woman came shortly after the guards had gone in one direction. And I throw up a little ball of paper to her sitting up on the back of somebody else. Which I said, [NON-ENGLISH], I said so she should stop. [NON-ENGLISH]. So she stopped and then I threw this paper out and other people threw papers out, too. And she picks them up, and the guard came back again, after he had [INAUDIBLE]. And now I knew that she had done some thinking about it. Now [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the nice thing was that I heard my name called. [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], means Jew who is in protective custody, Perl, it was my title [NON-ENGLISH]. So I went to see what it is. This must already have to do with these pieces of paper. And there was a Viennese police officer there. I forgot his name now. But it's in my book.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he asked me, we have here a man, he says, do you know Mr. Diamand? I said, I know several Diamands. It's a common Jewish name. He said, do you know a Mr. so and so Diamand? I think I said, I don't know. But I know him. I don't know him very well. But it's very possible that I know him. He said, he claims that you have his passport. Now I said, this is the place where you have to make a decision. And I told him, I have not one passport. I have several hundred passports. He said, what do you say? I said, I have several hundred passports. What are you? A passport forger? How come you have several hundred passports?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, because I am organizing the mass immigration of Jews from Palestine in cooperation with the German authorities in Berlin. He said, wait a moment. And he called Mr. Eichmann. And the lives of many people was saved by Eichmann not being in. Because Eichmann knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he called Eichmann's Superior, [INAUDIBLE] Lange. L-A-N-G-E, who was the chief of the Gestapo, I think, for Vienna. Anyhow, Eichmann was only a second lieutenant at the time. But he was in charge of Viennese Jews. And he told Lange, I have here a man who claims that he is organizing a mass immigration of Jews to Palestine. What should I do with him? And he said that-- yes, first he called Deutsche. And he said, I'll check you out with Eichmann. I'll check you out with the German authorities. I said, it's not only I who works on it. My good coworker, Turi Deutsche works on it too. And Turi Deutsche was in the courtyard at that time. And he was called up. And he was coming in very amazed. And he saw me sitting there. And this police officer asked him, do you know this man? He says, yes. Who is he? He is Dr. Perl. What do you know about him? He didn't know what to do. He said, he's a lawyer, and he's a Zionist. He says, what else do you know about him? And Turi hesitated. So I said, tell the truth about what we are preparing. So now he came with the truth. And the whole transport will not go off if I'm not released. So he released us. And I tried to get the first guy whom I knew from the Betar, the Betar leader. [INAUDIBLE]. I said, Goldstein I need too. So he said, Goldstein, Goldfarb, Goldschmidt. There's enough of these Jews. And the two of us were released with the order to report to Dr. [? Rayak-- ?] to report to Eichmann about-- I don't remember to report to whom about the details of our transport. So we were outed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I called now Dr. [? Rayakovich. ?] How did I get to [? Rayakovich? ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes. I went now to the [? Quakers. ?] And the [? Quakers-- ?] nobody-- there was a [? Quaker ?] representative there. The Viennese didn't even know what [? Quakers ?] are. His name was-- I forgot his name. It doesn't come to my mind right now. And I told him we are preparing a transport to get the Jews out. And they arrested us, and we are supposed to be back-- to be back here on this and this day. I told him-- I think I told him, the little lieutenant, that the transport is going to leave within four days. So within four days after the transport, that's what he want.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the transport leaves, he asked, when does the transport leave? So I told him, on this and this day, four days, at so and so, at such a time-- we didn't have any idea yet-- such and such time from this and this railroad station, so that he shouldn't inquire. So I had now in the meanwhile we went to [INAUDIBLE] was the name of this representative of the [? Quakers. ?] And the Jews did not know him, didn't know what the [? Quakers ?] are. They were very suspicious. The rumor was that there were two [INAUDIBLE], a Gestapo man who pretends to be friends to the Jews, and then that was the rumor. But actually his main assignment was that Jews were converted to Christian. But nobody took care, the [INAUDIBLE] had nothing to do with them. And they were considered Jews, and he worked for them. He tried to get them out. And I told him about it. And he called [? Rayakovich. ?] And told [? Rayakovich ?] about the story. [? Rayakovich ?] was the son-in-law of [? Irentelel. ?] [? Irentelel, ?] I-R-E-N-T-E-L-E-L, was an Austrian Secretary of something in the cabinet of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dollfuss, I guess?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Dollfuss, yeah. Who had taken part in the preparation of the murder of Dollfuss, a Nazi, a Nazi. And he was now a big man in the Nazi movement. And he caused-- the [INAUDIBLE] called [? Rayakovich ?], who was the son-in-law of this well-known Nazi. And told him about it. And he says, you can go and see [? Rayakovich ?] on this and this day. So we went to [? Rayakovich ?]. And we had-- like this here, a little briefcase, [INAUDIBLE], with some 200 passports in it. And we started talking to [? Rayakovich. ?] Turi pushed the button and by chance, these things, emptied. And he was impressed by all the passports. This was right at the beginning. He didn't know. So we told him the transport is to leave on this and this day. Again, a certain day that we give him. He said, OK. I'll arrange. He called Lange. He called Lange. And Lange said, they should get a transport out. They should get a transport out. But this was already-- we had two or three weeks in between now. But they have to report to the [? Karajan, ?] because they are registered there as inmates. First you'll there on this and this day, it was a day or two later at this and this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we reported on this and this day at this and this time. And we sold access to the [? Karajan, ?] the school was for build closed by the police, [INAUDIBLE]. But he says we have to go in there. There was no use. You crazy to go there if you didn't have to. So we went there. We went there, and they were just loading the buses with people for Dachau. And as we came in, and we said, we want to speak to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lange, or police for Lange, whatever his rank was. And they said, shut up, and pushed us into the group of people who were to go. We were lined up waiting to be shipped. And here, I saw in real life, a Jew was murdered. It was the only thing I saw personally.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jew there who suffered of epilepsy. And he fell. We had to line up in military formation, two long rows. And he fell in his formation. And then an SS man came and shouted, get up [NON-ENGLISH]. And he didn't get up. So he kicked him with his black, heavy boot, with all his strength. And he still didn't get up. So he kicked him again, and blood came out of his mouth. And he kicked him until the man couldn't move anymore. I'm sure he was dead. And then Eichmann came in, accompanied by one or two-- I don't remember anymore-- of his adjutants, with a little [NON-ENGLISH], a little cane, like a cane, a [INAUDIBLE] stick or something, [NON-ENGLISH.] And the other people already lined up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, all of you, he said, I want to know who volunteers for the shipment to Dachau. And one Jew got out. And Eichmann ask him, why do you volunteer for Dachau? He said, because I want to get it over with. He said, you all of you will get it over with. And you all are going to Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in line, we were to be freed. So I waited. They shipped two or four, I don't remember, in each little group for the bus to take them to the railroad station. And when our group came, I left the group and went to Lange, who was standing there. And told him-- well, first I told the sergeant. I have to report to Lange.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, shut up, or something like this. And so I told him, this is an important matter. This on you. I told him in commander's tone. I have to report-- from the beginning-- I have to report to Lange. He said, what is it? I told him, I can't tell you. It's confidential. I'll tell him. So I said, you will be responsible if there is any mix up. I have to report to Lange. So he brought us to Lange, and he said these Jews want to-- Turi was with me-- want to report to you. And Lange says, what is it? And we said, if you spoke to Dr. [? Rayakovich, ?] and we are going to-- he said, oh yes. Let them go. And they should report within-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, there was already not a time cap. And you keep contact with us regarding the dispatch of the [INAUDIBLE], of the ship. And we came home. And my brother was come-- I forgot to tell this-- to the prison while I was still there. When I came out, he was out there. He was out there waiting for me. Because [INAUDIBLE] had brought a message to him. And I was with him, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what was the next thing? We're going to have to pause now and change tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I think I'll have to eat now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. We'll take--
